perturbation.original_text: b. The Confidential Information that must be protected under this Agreement in- cludes (i) tangible information (such as written materials, models, and/or speci- mens) identified as being Confidential Information by an appropriate, conspicuous legend (such as “Confidential” or “Proprietary”); (ii) information in oral or visual form that is identified as being Confidential Information at the time of disclosure and confirmed in writing as Confidential Information within fourteen (14) days after the disclosure; or (iii) information that, given the nature of the information or the circumstances surrounding its disclosure, reasonably should be considered to be Confidential Information
perturbation.changed_text: b. The Confidential Information that must be protected under this Agreement in- cludes (i) tangible information (such as written materials, models, and/or speci- mens) identified as being Confidential Information by an appropriate, conspicuous legend (such as “Confidential” or “Proprietary”); (ii) information in oral or visual form that is identified as being Confidential Information at the time of disclosure and confirmed in writing as Confidential Information within fourteen (14) days after the disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing clause (iii) from Section 2.b, which dictates that confidential Information shall include information that, given the nature of the information or the circumstances surrounding its disclosure, reasonably should be considered to be Confidential Information, creates ambiguity. This clause is key when an information is disclosed without any label given to it, since in some cases it is obvious that the information is confidential, due to the circumstances in where it was being disclosed, and by removing this clause and having the other clause intact, it creates an in-text contradiction. This creates ambiguity and uncertainty in enforcement, since the party receiving the information can claim that there was no signal that the shared information was confidential and proprietary. Section 8 then becomes harder to enforce since what qualifies as information to protect is not well defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Protection. Each Party agrees to protect Confidential Information provided to it by the Discloser with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential, proprietary, and/or trade secret information and shall promptly advise the Discloser, in writing, of any misappropriation or misuse, of which it becomes aware, of Confidential Information by any person.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined Confidential Information to include information that 'reasonably should be considered to be Confidential Information'. The changed text omits this clause, narrowing the definition of Confidential Information. This creates a contradiction with Section 8, which requires parties to protect 'Confidential Information' provided by the Discloser. Without the omitted clause, it's unclear whether information not explicitly marked or confirmed in writing, but reasonably understood as confidential, is subject to the protection obligation in Section 8. This introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability, constituting a strong in-text contradiction according to the provided definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Exception. If the Recipient is required by order of any court or other government or regulatory agency to disclose any Confidential Information belonging to any Dis- closer, it shall provide all Parties with prompt written notice within 3 business days of any such requirement so that the Parties whose Confidential Information is at risk may seek a protective order or take other appropriate action. Upon the request and at the expense of the requesting Party, the other Parties will reasonably cooperate with the requesting Party to obtain an appropriate protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information. Subject to the foregoing requirements, it shall not be a breach of this Agreement for a Recipi- ent to disclose Confidential Information if such disclosure is: (a) in response to a valid order of a court or governmental body of the United States or any political subdivision thereof; (b) otherwise required by law; or (c) necessary to establish the Discloser’s rights under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Exception. If the Recipient is required by order of any court or other government or regulatory agency to disclose any Confidential Information belonging to any Dis- closer, it shall provide all Parties with prompt written notice within 3 business days of any such requirement. Subject to the foregoing requirements, it shall not be a breach of this Agreement for a Recipi- ent to disclose Confidential Information if such disclosure is: (a) in response to a valid order of a court or governmental body of the United States or any political subdivision thereof; (b) otherwise required by law; or (c) necessary to establish the Discloser’s rights under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the text "so that the Parties whose Confidential Information is at risk may seek a protective order or take other appropriate action. Upon the request and at the expense of the requesting Party, the other Parties will reasonably cooperate with the requesting Party to obtain an appropriate protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information." it undermines the protection of Confidential Information, especially given that the definition of this Information was reduced in section 2. Now there is no instruction on the parties to act when that Confidential Information is at Risk, creating an in-text contradiction. The instruction on this section becomes hollow now, and contradicts with section 8.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8. Protection. Each Party agrees to protect Confidential Information provided to it by the Discloser with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential, proprietary, and/or trade secret information and shall promptly advise the Discloser, in writing, of any misappropriation or misuse, of which it becomes aware, of Confidential Information by any person.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 5 provided a mechanism for parties to protect Confidential Information by seeking protective orders or other assurances when disclosure was required by a court or agency. The changed text removes this mechanism, creating a direct contradiction with section 8, which requires each party to 'protect Confidential Information...with at least the same degree of care as it protects its own confidential, proprietary, and/or trade secret information'. Without the ability to seek protective orders, the obligation in section 8 to protect Confidential Information is weakened, especially since Section 2 definition of confidential information was reduced in another step. This creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Personnel. Each Party may use the same personnel who receive Confidential Infor- mation under this Agreement on other projects similar to the Purpose so long as such Party does not breach the confidentiality of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Personnel. Each Party may use the same personnel on other projects similar to the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "who receive Confidential Information under this Agreement so long as such Party does not breach the confidentiality of this Agreement," we weaken the restriction on personnel use. Now it contradicts the initial definition on section 2. With the initial phrase the contract was saying that all Personal that has acess to Confidential Information should not breach, creating a direct bond between personell and confidential information, with the removal it removes the initial intended definition creating an in-text contradiction. This weakens the protection of Confidential Information since there is no reference to it. Also, considering we reduced the definition of what is Confidential Information, it also makes the bond between the personell and the definition weaker.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Confidential Information. a. “Confidential Information” shall mean information and materials, whether in written, oral, visually, electronic or another format. The parties agree to exchange information as they deem appropriate for the Purpose. b. The Confidential Information that must be protected under this Agreement in- cludes (i) tangible information (such as written materials, models, and/or speci- mens) identified as being Confidential Information by an appropriate, conspicuous legend (such as “Confidential” or “Proprietary”); (ii) information in oral or visual form that is identified as being Confidential Information at the time of disclosure and confirmed in writing as Confidential Information within fourteen (14) days after the disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text of Section 9 restricted the use of personnel who received Confidential Information under the Agreement to projects similar to the Purpose, as long as confidentiality was maintained. The changed text removes the condition 'who receive Confidential Information under this Agreement so long as such Party does not breach the confidentiality of this Agreement'. This elimination contradicts Section 2, which defines Confidential Information and specifies how it must be protected. By removing the direct link between personnel and the confidentiality obligation, the change weakens the protection of Confidential Information, creating a conflict between the obligation to protect information in Section 2 and the now-unrestricted use of personnel in Section 9, because there are less direct restraints on the personnel.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Return of Information. Upon the written request of the Discloser, the Recipient shall either destroy or return to the Discloser any and all Confidential Information in the Recipient’s possession, except for one copy, which copy may be retained for archival purposes in a secure file. All copies of Confidential Information forwarded in error by any Discloser shall be destroyed in their entirety without the Recipient retaining an archival copy.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Return of Information. Upon the written request of the Discloser, the Recipient shall either destroy or return to the Discloser any and all information in the Recipient’s possession.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception that "except for one copy, which copy may be retained for archival purposes in a secure file. All copies of Confidential Information forwarded in error by any Discloser shall be destroyed in their entirety without the Recipient retaining an archival copy", we create a contradiction. While we stated that the Recipient should return/destroy all the information in it's possesion, it still lacks clarity on when this information was send in error, so the clause is missing. In the event of an error on the information being send, Section 2 is contradicted since now the party doesn't know how to handle information that might contain private data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Confidential Information. a. “Confidential Information” shall mean information and materials, whether in written, oral, visually, electronic or another format. The parties agree to exchange information as they deem appropriate for the Purpose. b. The Confidential Information that must be protected under this Agreement in- cludes (i) tangible information (such as written materials, models, and/or speci- mens) identified as being Confidential Information by an appropriate, conspicuous legend (such as “Confidential” or “Proprietary”); (ii) information in oral or visual form that is identified as being Confidential Information at the time of disclosure and confirmed in writing as Confidential Information within fourteen (14) days after the disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit exception for retaining an archival copy of confidential information and the instruction for destroying incorrectly forwarded information. This creates a contradiction with Section 2, which defines 'Confidential Information' and implies a need for proper handling and protection of such information. Without the explicit instruction to destroy incorrectly sent information, the recipient is left without a clear guideline, causing a direct conflict with the general directive to protect confidential information outlined in Section 2.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Indemnification. Each Party shall fully indemnify the other against any and all ac- tions, claims, liability, costs, damages, charges and expenses suffered or incurred in connection with or arising out of any breach by a Party of any of the provisions of this Agreement, or by any unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information by a third party or by any employee of any Party to whom Confidential Information has been disclosed or who has been allowed access thereto. Each Party acknowledges and confirms that a breach of its obligations hereunder cannot be compensated adequately solely by an award of damages or indemnity or other pecuniary remedy, and that the non-breaching Party shall also be entitled in the event of any such breach to the rem-edies of injunction, specific performance, and/or other equitable relief in respect of any such breach. Nothing herein shall be construed as a waiver by either Party of any of its rights including rights to damages or indemnity or other pecuniary remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Indemnification. Each Party shall fully indemnify the other against any and all ac- tions, claims, liability, costs, damages, charges and expenses suffered or incurred in connection with or arising out of any breach by a Party of any of the provisions of this Agreement, or by any unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information by a third party or by any employee of any Party to whom Confidential Information has been disclosed or who has been allowed access thereto. Nothing herein shall be construed as a waiver by either Party of any of its rights including rights to damages or indemnity or other pecuniary remedy.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "Each Party acknowledges and confirms that a breach of its obligations hereunder cannot be compensated adequately solely by an award of damages or indemnity or other pecuniary remedy, and that the non-breaching Party shall also be entitled in the event of any such breach to the rem-edies of injunction, specific performance, and/or other equitable relief in respect of any such breach." weakens the indemnification clause. This creates uncertainty on the remedies one party can take against the other, since only damages and costs are referenced. This contradicts with the clause in Section 15 since it says “Each Party agrees that the wrongful disclosure of Confidential Information may cause irreparable injury that is inadequately compensable by monetary damages.”
perturbation.contradicted_text: 15. Injunctive Relief. Each Party agrees that the wrongful disclosure of Confidential In- formation may cause irreparable injury that is inadequately compensable by monetary damages. As such, either Party may seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction for the breach and/or the threatened breach of this Agreement in addition to any other remedies it may seek at law or equity, and the Recipient will not raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that breaches of the agreement might not be adequately compensated by monetary damages alone, allowing for remedies like injunction and specific performance. The changed text removes this statement. Section 15 explicitly states that monetary damages are inadequate for wrongful disclosure of confidential information and allows for injunctive relief. By removing the acknowledgment that monetary damages might be insufficient in Section 16, the changed text weakens the possibility of seeking remedies beyond monetary compensation, which directly contradicts Section 15's assertion that such damages are inadequate and that injunctive relief is available. This is a strong contradiction because it creates uncertainty about the available remedies for a breach, impacting enforceability.
